title: Missing no_grad During Inference
description:
## The Situation

Your embedding generation job crashes after processing about 200 batches:

```
RuntimeError: CUDA out of memory.
Try using torch.no_grad() during inference.
```

The model itself fits in VRAM with room to spare. But memory keeps climbing with every single batch, even though you are not training — no `loss.backward()`, no optimizer step. Something is keeping tensors alive.

## Your Task

1. Connect to the server (you already have a terminal)
2. Read `embed.py` — understand why memory grows during a plain forward pass
3. Add the fix that stops PyTorch from building computation graphs during inference
4. Click **Check Solution** to verify your fix

## Useful Commands

```bash
python3 embed.py      # run the script (crashes without fix)
cat embed.py          # view the code
nano embed.py         # edit the code
nvidia-smi            # watch GPU memory
```
hints: During training, PyTorch builds a computation graph for every forward pass so it can calculate gradients later. During inference you never call .backward(), so this graph is wasted memory that just accumulates.
PyTorch has a context manager specifically for inference that disables gradient tracking for everything inside it. When active, no computation graphs are built and memory stays flat across batches.
Wrap the model forward call inside the loop with `with torch.no_grad():`. This one addition stops the graph from being built on each iteration.
